paper_title: Public–Private Dialogs to Spur Export-led Growth: The Case of Productivity Taskforces in Namibia
research_answer: Namibia imports the vast majority of its fruit consumption — only 4% of produce was sourced locally in 2019 — and just 17% of its total arable land was under cultivation in 2021, pointing to a large untapped productive base. Yet the export performance of high-value fruits has been accelerating rapidly: grape exports rose 40% between 2020 and 2022 and another 21% in 2023, while two high-value fruit farms ranked among Namibia's four fastest-growing companies in 2023. Investment signals are also strong: a US$80 million blueberry project backed by two large Spanish agri-food companies was announced in 2023, targeting a 250-hectare farm and aimed at exporting its first harvest in September 2024. Taken together, the picture is of a sector still in early stages — with most land uncultivated and most fruit still imported — but showing the kind of export acceleration and inward investment that can indicate the beginning of a structural transformation, especially in the context of global demand for grapes, dates, and blueberries rising consistently since the early 2000s.
supporting_quotes: Only 4% of the produce was sourced locally in 2019, and only 17% of the total arable land was under cultivation in 2021.
Gross exports increased by 40% between 2020 and 2022.
In 2023, gross exports of grapes increased by 21% versus the previous year.
Two high-value fruit farms were among the four top fastest-growing companies in Namibia, according to Africa's Fastest Growing Companies Report 2023.
A company focused on the production of blueberries (Namibia Berries) announced a US$80 million investment over seven years to establish a 250-hectare farm in the Kavango East region.
The global demand for grapes, dates, and blueberries had been consistently increasing since the early 2000s – with growing markets in the United States, Europe, and Asia.
